coredata.dc:title: The application of exosomes and Exosome-nanoparticle in treating brain disorders
coredata.dc:creator: Salarpour, Soodeh
Barani, Mahmood
Pardakhty, Abbas
Khatami, Mehrdad
Pal Singh Chauhan, Narendra
coredata.dc:description: Brain-derived exosomes (BDEs) are extracellular nanovesicles released by all central nervous system cell lineages and contain cargo from the cells that produced them. In physiological and pathological conditions, they are becoming more well-known as critical mediators of communication and waste management between neurons, glial cells, and connective tissue in the brain. It may provide a mechanistic view of the disease phenotype, useful for diagnosis and therapeutic intervention. We emphasize the importance of exosomes as a pathogenic agent and a disease biomarker in Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), Multiple Sclerosis (MS), and Glioblastoma. It is because exosomes have the ability to cross the blood–brain barrier, our understanding of these unique nanovesicles may provide not only answers about the neurodegenerative diseases but also ideal methods for developing these vesicles as drug delivery vehicles or tools to monitor brain diseases in a non-invasive manner. This review will discuss exosomes' physicochemical properties, extraction, and diagnosis functions in the brain and relate new findings to current evidence of exosome nanoparticles in brain disorders.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Exosome–cargo, surface markers, and endosomal pathway. This figure shows the process of exosome production and secretion. 1 Early endosome; 2 Late endosomes; 3 Multivesicular body; following on, the multivesicular body will fuse with the cell membrane to secrete exosomes out of the cell or fuse with the lysosome [41].

Schematic representation of the endocytic pathway. Membrane proteins (shown in red) internalized through clathrin-coated vesicles are delivered to early endosomes. In early endosomes, molecules are recycled to the plasma membrane or sequestered in internal vesicles. Early morphological observations revealed that the internal vesicles of MVBs are generated by budding from the limiting membrane into the lumen of endosomes. In the degradation pathway, MVBs fuse with lysosomes. In several hematopoietic and nonhematopoietic cells, MVBs fuse with the plasma membrane, in which case the internal vesicles of the MVB are released into the extracellular medium as exosomes. Exosomes display the same orientation as the plasma membrane, with extracellular domains of proteins exposed at the surface and enclosing a droplet of cytoplasm, The reference [42]. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic representation of isolating exosomes by differential ultracentrifugation. All centrifugations are carried out at 4⁰C. The reference [52].
Graphic illustration of Au NPs coated with exosome for brain targeting [141].
Schematic representation for incorporating fluorescent dyes and AuNP-PEG-FA into EVs generated from B16F10 cells [146].

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Brain-derived exosomes (BDEs) are extracellular nanovesicles released by all central nervous system cell lineages and contain cargo from the cells that produced them. In physiological and pathological conditions, they are becoming more well-known as critical mediators of communication and waste management between neurons, glial cells, and connective tissue in the brain. It may provide a mechanistic view of the disease phenotype, useful for diagnosis and therapeutic intervention. We emphasize the importance of exosomes as a pathogenic agent and a disease biomarker in Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), Multiple Sclerosis (MS), and Glioblastoma. It is because exosomes have the ability to cross the blood–brain barrier, our understanding of these unique nanovesicles may provide not only answers about the neurodegenerative diseases but also ideal methods for developing these vesicles as drug delivery vehicles or tools to monitor brain diseases in a non-invasive manner. This review will discuss exosomes' physicochemical properties, extraction, and diagnosis functions in the brain and relate new findings to current evidence of exosome nanoparticles in brain disorders.

ce:section.ce:para: Drug delivery to the brain is one of the most exciting and challenging fields in pharmaceutical research. Drug development in brain disorders has the poorest success rates than other therapeutic organs. The brain has been separated from blood circulation and other body parts by a substantial barrier composed of complex tight junctions [1]. This barricade, the blood–brain barrier (BBB), limits the accumulation and passage of molecules into the central nervous system (CNS) to protect it from microorganisms, neurotoxins, and chemical substances. The BBB has a complex system and transfers molecules selectively [2]. The transport of molecules through BBB depends on their size, chemical composition, surface properties, and other factors. Only small molecules which are lipophilic and have a molecular weight < 400 Da could transfer through the BBB [3]. Most small therapeutic molecules and almost all macromolecules such as recombinant peptides, proteins, antibodies, plasmids, and RNAs cannot cross the BBB. For this reason, the BBB poses an obstacle for the direct delivery of therapeutic molecules which are highly effective to CNS-associated illnesses like Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s diseases, multiple sclerosis, stroke, brain tissue malignancies or cancers, and finally, microbial infections [4,5]. When effective concentrations of therapeutic molecules couldn’t reach the brain, the concentration elevation causes adverse effects in other areas. The targeted therapy is many points in developing drugs to decrease treatment strategies' side effects.

As a challenge, scientists have tried to employ protocols for the delivery of drugs across BBB. In this way, nanotechnology can overcome the limitation associated with BBB [6]. Nano drug delivery systems include different structural natures such as lipid, polymer, metal/inorganic, and biologic based nanoparticles that could be modified to develop their properties to achieve a better drug release profile, such as targeting delivery [7]. Nanomedicines or nano-drug delivery systems are in nano-size range scale with the possibility to cross the BBB and increase retention time in the circulatory system [8-10]. These systems could have the appropriate capacity for loading molecules and releasing them with controlled conditions, so the nanostructure systems have been studied for theranostic purposes [11-15]. Also, some simulation can be performed to design more effective systems [16,17].

Polymeric nanoparticles like PBCA [18] and PLGA nanoparticles were studied for the treatment of Alzheimer’s disease [19,20] and Parkinson’s disease [21]. Carbon dots [22,23], lipid-based nanoparticles such as solid lipid nanoparticles [24,25], liposomes [26,27], niosomes [28,29], and metallic nanoparticles (like silver and gold) [30-32] have been evaluated for drug delivery across the BBB.

Nevertheless, nano-drug delivery systems have some pitfalls such as toxicity, accumulation of nanomaterials such as polymers in the CNS, and immunogenicity, especially when their administrations are repeated. These nano-sized particles could be rapidly cleared by the reticuloendothelial system (RES) or the mononuclear phagocyte system. Accordingly, the number of nano-drug delivery systems approved by the FDA isn’t many [33,34]. The techniques like PEGylation of the surface of nanoparticles can help extend their circulation in the body. Still, the PEGylation could hinder the interaction of drugs with the target area [33]. Therefore, endogenous vesicles such as extracellular vesicles (EVs) have been utilized as drug delivery vehicles [23,35]. Extracellular vesicles are categorized into three types (exosomes, ectosomes, and apoptotic bodies): different in size, origin, and synthesis. The exosomes are the smallest of them and are sized in nanoscale. The protein and peptides of exosomes could administer to illnesses (or disorders) like cancers type, viral infections, inflammation, and neurodegenerative diseases [36].

Exosomes could be utilized as natural nano-drug delivery vehicles in which desired therapeutic molecules could be loaded into them. Exosomes have specifications that are ideal properties for drug delivery. They contain protein and genetic materials that transfer these molecules between cells and deliver them by crossing the plasma membrane. These biological cargoes could be loaded into exosomes as delivery vehicles. Exosomes are distributed widely in body fluids; hence it might be indicative exosomes could be well tolerated and circulate for a long time in the body. The other feature of exosomes that is beneficial for targeting drug delivery is membrane modification. It has been studied overexpression of a fusion gene for encoding specific membrane protein to more intense targeting [37].

Exosomes and small unilamellar vesicle (SUV)-type liposomes are vesicular structures made up of a single lipid bilayer with mean dimensions ranging from 50 to 120 nm. The main difference between exosomes and SUV-liposomes is the complex surface composition and the characteristic array of tetraspanins proteins located at the exosome membrane. Proteins are rarely found in or on the lipid bilayer of SUV-liposome. The tetraspanins proteins presrecipientsome facilitate effective targeting and absorption by recipient cells. Due to their similarities, SUV-liposomes are frequently used as a control vesicle formulation compared to exosomes in drug delivery applications [38].

In this literature, we describe exosomes properties and methods for isolating these vesicles. The application of exosomes in diagnosis in brain disorders and as a new drug delivery system to deliver active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) to the brain are presented.

Exosomes are nanoscale vesicles with densities between 1.13 and 1.19 g/mL consisting of two layers of fat membranes with a predominantly spherical shape and a diameter below 100 nm [39]. In reported articles, exosomes are generally 40 to 150 nm in size. Processes like exocytosis of Live cells naturally observe large sizes up to 200 nm and smaller to 30 nm. Exosomes are heterogeneous in size, even for exosomes secreted from a cell line [40]. Exosomes are formed by germination from the plasma membrane and the multivesicular endosomes (MVEs). Exosomes can be produced by direct vesicle budding from the surface of cells or by vesicle budding into endosomes, with subsequent release upon endosomes (Fig. 1 ).

More giant vesicles (up to 1000 nm) or smaller (up to 1 nm) exosome sizes are rapidly removed from the systemic circulation, and small vesicles pass quickly through the vessel wall. Exosomes are becoming the main focus of targeted drug delivery studies due to their constant presence in the circulatory system and protection of their contents against various proteases and nucleases. Exosomes can pass through biological membranes due to their small size. These nano-lipids can bind and carry proteins and RNAs. The compounds inside the exosomes are protected from decomposition and can transfer various compounds to the cell. Exosomes have been introduced as a new agent for drug delivery and treatment of diseases due to their valuable properties, such as non-stimulation of the immune system, specificity of the receptor, and transport of drug compounds. The ability to store and transmit biological information has made exosomes a powerful tool for biomedical applications. Exosomes can be the carriers of multicellular organisms and deliver letters, which are the same biological information, to the target cells. Inactivation of the immune system, the ability to cross the blood–brain barrier, high stability in biological fluids, high specific function for binding to target cells, the ability to engineer them to package various compounds are the essential advantages that have led to the use of exosomes for treatment.

Due to their intracellular origin, exosome lipid vesicles (Fig. 2 ) are more stable than synthetic polymers and liposomes. They can remain in the body longer, thus being an alternative strategy for targeted drug delivery.

Exosomes derived from oligodendrocytes contain myelin sheath components, myelin lipid protein proteins, myelin proteins, and myelin glycoproteins. Exosomes secreted by antigen-presenting cells contain high MHC class II proteins, whereas exosomes released by oligodendrocytes lack this protein. Exosomes have different cell origins and different constituents. Exosomes produced by nerve cells can penetrate the axons of adjacent cells to transmit the message to other cells. Therefore, depending on their place of origin, exosomes have unique characteristics that indicate the pathophysiological status of the cell that produces them. Hence the exosomes are the fingerprints of the mother cells.

Exosomes contain RNAs transported to activate a functional response in cells and other tissues. The study showed that exosomes derived from stem cells had RNA, which increased the expression of the protein-encoding the same RNA in its receptor cells. This demonstrates the ability of RNA-based information to be transferred from one cell to another via exosomes [43]. Exosomes also contain DNA, and these DNAs appear to indicate viral disease or cancer.

Identification of exosome vesicles is based on morphological and biochemical criteria. Exosomes can only be seen with an electron microscope due to their small size. Exosome membranes contain phosphatidylcholine (PC), phosphatidylserine (PS), phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), phosphatidylinositol (PIs), phosphatidic acid (PA), cholesterol, ceramides, sphingomyelin, glycine. Exosomes are rich in PE and PS in their outer layer membrane. The concentration of lipids in the membrane of exosomes differs from other membranes of organelles and cell membranes, indicating the cell and the specific path of their biogenesis. The use of perfengolysin toxin has been shown to exosomes rich in cholesterol [44].

Rab proteins are one of the most critical proteins in the production of exosomes. Studies have shown that cells that are mutated in the process of Rab proteins and have lost the function of this protein are up to 70% less able to produce exosomes than healthy cells. Some studies have suggested that Rab proteins may be involved in the maturation and transport of exosomes. Inhibition of Ral GTPase expression also reduced the half-life of cell exosomes.

Exosomes contain many membrane proteins, membrane-bound proteins, and exosomal lumen-soluble proteins. Studies have shown that exosomes are rich in membrane proteins belonging to the tetraspanin family. Among this family, CD81 protein is the most abundant in the exosome and has been used as an exosome cell marker [45].
The presence of several heat shock proteins in exosomes has been identified. Exosomes naturally contain part of the cytoplasm of the source cell due to their biogenesis [46].

A wide range of enzymes in exosomes has been reported due to one of their inherent functions of transmitting information between cells. The membrane and internal contents of exosomes are not always constant. They change due to changes in the expression of disease-causing genes, the effects of hormones, nutrition, and other influential environmental factors.

One of the critical properties of exosomes is increasing the stability and durability of the compounds encapsulated in the circulatory system. Chemotherapeutic drugs such as doxorubicin (paclitaxel, paclitaxel, celastrol) and curcumin have been loaded into exosomes and treated for cancer.

The capacity of exosomes to target cancer cells is about 10 times greater than that of liposomes of the same size, often targeting cancer cells more strongly than liposomes due to ligand-acceptor interactions on the receptor cells [47-49].

This feature dramatically improves the therapeutic effects of drugs. Studies had shown that curcumin encapsulated in exosomes increased blood plasma curcumin concentrations up to 10 times when curcumin was used alone.

The antitumor effects of curcumin have been shown in in vitro studies, but its poor solubility is a significant barrier to its appropriate clinical outcomes. After encapsulation, the curcumin in exosomes increased bioavailability, and its antitumor effects improved.

One of the properties of exosomes is their ability to kill drug-resistant cells. Their ability to overcome drug resistance may be due to entry through endocytosis, which in turn prevents the release of drug agents by glycoprotein P. Such studies well demonstrate the ability of exosomes to deliver chemotherapeutic drugs to cancer cells and cells resistant to various therapies, and on the other hand, show an increased ability of loaded medications to kill cancer cells compared to free drugs [50].

Based on the innate desire of the exosomes, the researchers loaded brain-derived neurotrophic factors into macrophage-derived exosomes to target diseases of the central nervous system. In the in vivo study, loaded exosomes accumulated in the brain tissue about 6 times more and entered the brain 3 times faster in inflammatory conditions than in normal conditions. These results indicate that exosomes are promising and suitable nanocarriers targeting brain diseases.

mRNA-rich exosomes can show functional expression of carrier mRNA proteins in vivo and have fewer side effects than doses equal to lipid nanoparticles. Exosomes, therefore, become candidates for the development of an mRNA-based, multifunctional vaccine that produces the Spike virus protein. This vaccine transmits long-term cellular and humoral responses to Nucleocapsid and Spike and effectively combats COVID-19 and other infectious diseases [51].

Exosomes are essential and perform many biological functions, especially cell-to-cell communication and the transfer of biological information. Exosomes can act as valuable biomarkers in diagnosing the disease. Therefore, it is necessary to discover and develop methods for separating and purifying exosomes. Rapid and efficient separation of exosomes is challenging due to the complexity of biological samples and other extracellular vesicles. With the rapid advancement of science and technology, various techniques have been developed to separate exosomes. Special features such as density, shape, size, and surface proteins, must be considered to separate exosomes. Identifying and studying the composition and properties of exosomes is directly related to the researcher's ability to separate and purify exosomes. This section provides an overview of exosome separation techniques and explores new perspectives for developing efficient exosome separation strategies and innovative methods to save more time.

The most commonly used and reported techniques in exosome separation are centrifuge-based and is the most standard method in exosome separation, and is the most widely used technique by researchers. Exosomes can be easily separated from cell culture by ultracentrifugation (Fig. 3 ). When a heterogeneous mixture (suspension) of different cell contents is centrifuged, the suspended particles in the suspension will settle to varying rotations of the centrifuge depending on the density, size, and shape. Heavy or larger particles settle sooner. The separation of exosomes by centrifugation usually involves a series of centrifuge cycles. Initial sample centrifuges are used to separate large particles from exosomes, and the supernatant is separated and centrifuged at higher cycles. Finally, the isolated exom specimens are kept at minus 80° C [52].

The most important and widely used technique for separating exosomes is differential ultracentrifugation, which involves a series of centrifuge cycles with different times for deposition of other cellular compounds; finally, a centrifuge with 100.000 to 120.000 lead to exosomes precipitates.

Exosomes are also centrifuged by centrifugation along the concentration gradient. Exosomes are on the slope of the parity error as long as their density is the same. Gradient materials such as cesium chloride are used for density gradient centrifuges.

The speed is one of the critical parameters in the secretion of pure exospore. Two methods were used to separate exosomes derived from mesenchymal stem cells at 100,000 g and 60,000 g. The results showed that the exosomes were purer at 100,000 g, but at 60,000 g, larger particles of the exosome, such as microcycles, may be seen [52].

The advantages of a centrifuge are ease of use, requiring very little technical expertise, and cost-effectiveness over time. One of the disadvantages of separation by ultracentrifugation is that due to the heterogeneity of the size of the exosomes and the significant overlap in the size of other extracellular vesicles, it leads to exosomal losses and contamination with other overlapping cellular compounds. Ultimately, this method depends on the use of expensive centrifuges.
Filtration based on the separation of suspended particles depends on their size. The separation of exosomes by these methods is faster than ultracentrifuges and does not require special equipment.

To separate the exosomes by filtration, sequential filtration of the studied suspension is used by membrane filters or porous chromatographic phases. To separate the exosomes in cell culture, the filter first separates the floating cells and the remnants of the large cells and does not allow large particles to pass through the filter, while particles smaller than the size of the cells can pass through the filter. Finally, the sample passed through the previous processes is filtered with a 100 nm filter. Western blotting was used to evaluate the quantity and purity of the isolated exosomes and showed that the exosomes were separated by appropriate purity by filtration.
One of the most critical limitations of filtration is the clogging and trapping of pores by vesicles, which reduces the separation efficiency and shortens its lifespan.

A technique with less power and cost has been used effectively to purify exosomes. For example, in one study, exosomes in urine, a cell-free sample, were inserted into a dialysis tube. The sample is trapped inside the dialysis tube at low solvent hydrostatic pressure, and small solutes pass freely through the pores of the dialysis tube. In contrast, extracellular vesicles containing exosomes are trapped in the dialysis tube. In this study, the exosome was 100 times more concentrated in the urine, and the sample was centrifuged and isolated for further purification. This technique effectively purifies the exosomes with much less force and cost.

A size-dependent microfluidic chip has been developed and used to separate exosomes from human blood by tangential flow filtration. A microfluidic chip was designed symmetrically with two polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) layers with serpentine channels and a pore polycarbonate membrane (PCTE) with pores 100 nm in diameter. Using PCTE membranes, exosomes trapped in the microfluidic chip can be collected by reverse washing with high recovery. Compared to centrifuges, which are considered the “gold standard” for exosome separation, the microfluidic chip-based protocol shows very high efficiencies in removing protein contaminants and a very high rate of exosome recovery. The whole process takes 3 h [53].

The presence of protein in the exosome membrane creates a unique opportunity to develop particular separation techniques based on the antigen–antibody approach and the interaction between receptors and ligands. This method is based on exosome adsorption and is based on safety techniques. Membrane-limited exosomal biomarkers that exist only on the surface of the studied exosomes are used for purification. The results show that purification with this method requires less sample volume, and purification of exomes with this method is better than methods such as centrifugation.

This type of separation technique, which requires several sample preparation steps, has time-consuming and tedious steps. One of the most effective methods of purifying exosomes is magnetic nanoparticles attached to antibodies to exosomal biomarkers. The antibody is attached to magnetic nanoparticles and placed in cellular or non-cellular suspension containing an exosome in this method. The antibody binds to the exosomal biomarker and separates the nanoparticles by the magnetic field. As a result, antibody-bound exosomes are isolated along with the nanoparticles. This method provides us with a technique for obtaining very pure samples of exosomes (Table 1 ).

CD63 on the exosome surface provides an attractive biomarker for separating exosomes from complex sample matrices by antibodies. Magnetic beads coated with anti-CD326 antibodies were used to detect the tumor. The epithelial cell adhesion molecule (CD326) is abundantly produced on the surface of the tumor exosome. The ability of this method to isolate the exosome means the presence of the tumor exosome in the sample and ultimately the diagnosis of cancer.

Another method is based on polyethylene glycol, a polymer that traps water molecules and thus causes compounds with less solubility to separate from the solution. In this method, the sample is incubated with a solution containing polyethylene glycol and then placed at four degrees for one day. Then, the sediment containing exosomes is separated by filtration or centrifugation at low speed—other methods, such as ultrafiltration, separate exosomes by size. The immunophilin technique isolates the use of specific antibodies based on exoskeleton membrane markers, and a centrifugal gradient can also be used.

The CNS has diverse barriers, including the blood-cerebrospinal fluid barrier, the BBB, the blood-retinal barrier, and the blood–spinal cord barrier. These barriers have various degrees of permeability, but BBB's most extensive and restricted. The BBB comprises tightly connected endothelial cells and a discontinuous layer of pericytes and astrocytes. Due to the integrity of the BBB, other cellular components like microglia and neurons communicate with BBB cells. The cerebral endothelial has a crucial role in the barrier function of BBB. The expression of specific proteins, adherent junctions, and tight junctions (like zonula occludens, claudins, occludin, etc.) strengthen the cohesiveness of the barrier [4,54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exosomes as drug delivery systems in brain disorders
ce:section.ce:para: CNS disorders like neurodegenerative diseases, strokes, and cancers are the critical cause of disability globally. The treatment of these diseases has many challenges [55].

Parkinson’s disease (PD) is one of the most common neurological disorders which primarily affect the motor system. The common clinical appearance of PD is resting tremor, bradykinesia (slowness of movement), muscular rigidity, and postural instability. In some cases, the disease started through non-motor symptoms like olfactory dysfunction, depression, sleep disturbances, gastrointestinal changes, and pain [56]. PD is a complex disorder that various factors cause it. The demise of dopaminergic neurons because of alpha-synuclein (α-Syn) aggregation is one of the factors of this disease. As dopamine neurons are degraded in the substantia nigra, the imbalance between excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters, respectively, occurs acetylcholine and dopamine [57]. Therefore, the administration of dopamine to treat PD is an option, but due to BBB, efficient delivery of dopamine to the brain is challenging. So, to improve the brain distribution of dopamine, different drug delivery systems were investigated [58]. Lewy bodies which are the misfolding and fibrillar aggregation of α-syn, are the essential pathological hallmark of PD. The α-syn encoded by the SNCA gene is the first genetic factor discovered for PD [56]. Also, mechanisms like reactive oxygen species (ROS), neuroinflammation, excitotoxicity, apoptosis, and loss of trophic factors are involved in PD. Although only one of the mechanisms may not be the generator of PD, molecular pathways act with each other to induce the degradation of dopamine neurons [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical therapeutical molecules loaded in exosomes
ce:section.ce:para: Mengke QU and their coworker’s utilized exosomes isolated from the blood to transfer dopamine to the brain. They loaded dopamine into exosomes with incubation of exosomes in a saturated solution of dopamine. They found exosomes could deliver dopamine to the brain (striatum and substantia nigra) better than free dopamine. Since the dopamine loaded in exosomes is more distributed in the brain, better therapeutic efficacy was seen in PD mouse models, and the systemic toxicity of dopamine was decreased [59].

In another investigation that researchers harnessed exosomes for PD treatment, a potent antioxidant like catalase was delivered to the brain. From there, PD is associated with brain inflammation and neurotoxic activity (reactive oxygen species (ROS)) and reduces the level of antioxidants such as redox enzymes, catalase, and superoxide dismutase. The effective delivery of antioxidants could help PD therapy [60].

This article selected exosomes secreted by monocytes and macrophages because the researchers believed these exosomes could escape from mononuclear phagocytes. For loading catalase into exosomes, incubation at room temperature, permeabilization with saponin, freeze–thaw cycles, sonication, and extraction methods were used. Exosomes were administered both intranasal and intravenous. It was considered exosomes diffused through the mouse brain (and localized predominantly in the cerebral frontal cortex, central sulcus, and cerebellum). Catalase-loaded exosomes significantly have neuroprotective effects in in-vitro and in vivo models of PD [61].

Exosome loaded with siRNA was another research that tried to delay the progression of PD. As mentioned before, the reduction of α-Syn aggregation that is the main component of Lewy bodies could be a protocol to treat PD. Syn small interfering RNA (siRNA) was loaded in exosomes in this research. The modified exosomes expressing Rabies Virus Glycoprotein (RVG) were applied/utilized.
In both standard and transgenic mice, it was found that exhibited aggregation of α-Syn, the α-Syn messenger RNA (mRNA) and protein levels throughout the brain decreased [62,63].

Among researchers in the field of PD models, drug loading in exosomes with the genetic modification method introduced above was evaluated by encoding a potent antioxidant enzyme through transfecting macrophage with pDNA [64].

In another research again RVG peptide was used in order to modified exosomes for specific delivery to brain. The DNA aptamer which was delivered with this modified exosome reduced the α-Syn so could reduce synaptic protein loss and dopaminergic neuronal death. The in vivo investigation of this research confirmed that the neuropathological and motor impairments alleviated through administration of RVG-exosomes contained aptamer [65].

As mentioned, down regulation of α-Syn is a point of PD therapy thus the shRNA minicircles was designed for this purpose also to improve its targeting again the RVG-exosomes was used in a study [66]. Another way that can use to reduce α-Syn is antisense oligonucleotides and exosomes were applied for transfer of them to brain [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combination of chemical and nucleotide loaded in exosomes
ce:section.ce:para: Hybrid of core containing curcumin/phenylboronic acid-poly(2-(dimethylamino)ethyl acrylate) nanoparticle/small interfering RNA targeting SNCA with the sell of exosome which modified by RVG peptide was introduced as nano-scavenger system. The siRNA targeting SNCA could down-regulate α-syn protein synthesis, hence this nano-scavenger could use for decreasing α-syn aggregation as well as reducing immune sensitivity due to exosome coating [68].

Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a neurodegenerative disorder that results in cognitive and behavioral impairment. It is the most prevalent form of dementia within the elderly population but these days in modern lifestyle younger stage AD appears. AD is not only dementia in meaning of losing memory AD is also complex of other symptoms such as thinking and behavioral disability, cognitive memory impairment, mood fluctuation, difficulty in communication, disturbance in a daily routine activity and changes in personality. The reasons of AD aren’t clearly defined, but genetic, mental stress, food, lifestyle, and similar factors are considered to be effective. The pathophysiology of AD is attributed to different hypotheses include, Amyloid cascade Cholinergic, Excitotoxicity, Mitochondrial cascade, and Tau hypothesis; also other reasons like genetic factors, inflammatory responses, free radical or oxidative stress, nitric oxide or toxins, environmental factors, and so on are suggested [69]. Some mechanisms such as BBB disruption, impaired glucose transport, decreased cerebral blood flow, and increased capillary tortuosity with altered rheology are regarding AD cerebrovascular dysfunctions [55]. According to scientific evidence, the acetylcholinesterase activity and glutamatergic system have important rules on AD pathology. Consequently, few drugs’ formulations based on the acetylcholinesterase activity and glutamatergic system have been approved but they have not good pharmacokinetic profiles [70].

As all therapeutic molecules for controlling AD progress or symptoms must affect centrally in the brain, the currently therapies confront with several limitations. The drug molecules have low bioavailability owing to compliance of brain structure especially permeability of BBB. For this purpose and to overcome these limitations, nanocarriers such as liposomes, nano-emulsions, dendrimers, solid lipid nanoparticles, and polymeric nanoparticles due to their properties have been utilized by researches. Beside the advantages of nanotechnology-based drug delivery systems that could transfer drugs to CNS better than drugs on its own, also intranasally administration of these systems to bypass the BBB has been suggested [71,72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical therapeutical molecules loaded in exosomes
ce:section.ce:para: Among nano drug delivery systems exosomes were also harnessed as drug carrier for AD treatment. Curcumin loaded exosomes were used to inhibit GSK-B which increased the Tau hyperphosphorylation. It was seen that extracellular inflammatory plaques formed by the deposition of amyloid-beta protein and neural fiber tangles in neuronal cells formed by phosphorylated Tau protein may cause the symptom (may be the main reason) of AD. In these exosome drug delivery systems that were utilized to AD therapy, curcumin was selected due to its regulation effect on Tau phosphorylation and exosomes extracted from mouse macrophage cells inherited the lymphocyte function-associated antigen 1 (LFA-1) peptides from their parent cells. This surface protein interacts with endothelial intercellular adhesion molecule 1 (ICAM-1) so mediates the migration of exosomes across the BBB.

In this research it was investigated that exosomes increase solubility and penetration of curcumin through BBB via receptor-mediated transcytosis. Also curcumin loaded exosomes inhibited Tau phosphorylation through activating AKT/GSK-3β pathway so better prevention of neurons death in vitro and in vivo was detected [73].

Quercetin is another herbal compound that was used for AD. It is a flavonoid that has neuroprotective, anti-oxidative, and anti-inflammatory effects. It was found quercetin loaded in plasma exosomes prevented Tau pathology more than free form of it, hence better symptoms of treatment for AD was achieved in exosomal formulation of quercetin [74].

As an example of exosomes utilization for specific drug targeting of nucleotides in AD, siRNA was delivered to the mice brain by dendritic cell derived exosomes. To reduce immunogenicity, the bone marrow from inbred C57BL/6 mice with a homogenous major histocompatibility complex (MHC) halotype was used to achieve exosomes. The dendritic cells were engineered to express lysosome-associated membrane glycoprotein 2b (Lamp2b) in order to target exosomes. Lamp2b is a protein on the surface of exosomes that fused to the neuro-specific RVG peptide.

GAPDH siRNA was loaded in these RVG-targeted exosomes by electroporation method. It was investigated RVG exosomes were not uptake specifically in other tissues. RVG exosomes loaded with siRNA after intravenous injection delivered GAPDH siRNA specifically to neurons microglia and oligodendrocytes and the knockdown of mRNA and protein BACE1 (β-Secretase APP Cleaving Enzyme 1) was observed(detected).

In order to (To) evaluation the therapeutic potential of exosomes that provided to transfer RNAs for treatment of AD, the effect of siRNA delivered by exosomes was examined. It was observed that BACE1 mRNA and protein were knockdowns in wild-type mice. BACE1 influence the β-cleavage of Amyloid Precursor Protein (APP) that produce the aggregation form of β-amyloid peptide so it may be a potential therapeutic target for treating AD [75,76].

Exosome containing RVG peptide on its surface for targeting α7-nAChR and simultaneously enriches a neprilysin variant was formulated by genetically changing of parental cells. In animal evaluation of this formulation the expression of proinflammatory genes, IL1α, TNFα and NF-κB was decreased and the expression of anti-inflammatory gene (IL10) was increased [77].

In treatment of brain inflammatory-related diseases like schizophrenia, meningitis, status epilepticus, migraine headaches and for inflammation in PD, AD, and tumors the standard anti-inflammatory drugs couldn’t be functional. This unfunctional drug delivery related to difficulty in penetrating the BBB so the effective concentration of therapeutic molecules couldn’t reach in brain [78]. Status epilepticus (SE) is a medical emergency, if it isn’t tranquilized, neurodegeneration, blood–brain barrier disruption, and inflammation in the hippocampus could be occurred. SE is curable by antiepileptic drugs but in extended SE when chronic hippocampus dysfunction, persistent inflammation and other relevant cascade pathologies cause regular epilepsy antiepileptic drugs couldn’t overcome the evolution of SE into chronic epilepsy [79].

In one study three inflammatory brain disease models, i.e., a lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced brain inflammation model, experimental autoimmune encephalitis and a GL26 brain tumor model were used to investigate the effect of intranasally delivery of curcumin or a signal transducer and activator of transcription 3 (Stat3) inhibitor, JSI-124 (cucurbitacin I), exosomes.

The mixing of curcumin or JSI124 with EL-4 cells derived exosomes in PBS were incubated at 22 °C for 5 min. Finally, these exosomes could protect mice brain from LPS-induction brain inflammatory and reduce the progression of myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein peptide which induced experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis, also inhibition of tumor growth in GL26 brain tumor model was detected [78].

It was suggested that modified exosomes with brain homing peptides could sensitize immune system. On the other hand, the cellular up take of naïve macrophages exosomes inherited LFA-1 from their parent cells could increase under inflammation condition due to this LFA-1 integrin mediate the interacts with ICAM-1 on endothelial cells which this adhesion molecule over expressed in inflammation condition. The LFA-1 is integrin that involve in migration and diapedesis of macrophages through the BBB (LFA-1: Lymphocyte Function-Associated antigen 1, ICAM-1: intercellular adhesion molecule 1) [80]. For these reasons, Yuan and their colleagues utilize naïve macrophage exosomes to transfer brain derived neurotrophic factors across the BBB for delivering to brain after IV administration and they find this delivery was further in inflamed brain condition [81].

The curcumin as an anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidative agent was loaded to mouse brain endothelial cells to achieve exosomes containing curcumin. This prime exosome formulation could improve junction proteins and endothelial cells permeability which damaged by high levels of homocysteine [82].

In order to reduce the adverse effects of status epilepticus the A1-exosomes which were isolated from human bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cells was investigated through intranasal administration. The loss of glutamatergic and GABAergic neurons and inflammation of hippocampus were decreased in mice that received A1-exosomes; hence the reduction of hippocampus inflammation has prevented cognitive and memory dysfunction of status epilepticus [79].

Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an inflammatory autoimmune disease of CNS involving demyelination, neurodegeneration, oligodendrocyte loss and sensibility to oxidative stress. Oligodendrocytes produce myelin around axons so damage to oligodendrocytes and demyelination cause impaired and slow nerve signal also disability of neural function. Therefore its symptoms could be physical, neurogical and psychological [83,84] and categorized in groups according to course and progression of the disease. The symptoms of MS could be: limb numbness like weakness of the face, visual field disturbance, double and blurred vision, paralysis, bladder/bowel problems, spasticity (stiffness and muscle spasms), and dizziness. The mechanism and causes involved in disease process are still unknown genetic and environmental factors (smoking, toxins), low vitamin D levels, obesity, microbial and viral infections, metabolism can contribute to recurrent immune attacks [85,86].

Pusic and their coworkers found that exosomes derived from rat serum (both young and environmental enrichment) contain high level of miR-219 that is necessary and sufficient for oligodendrocyte precursor cell differentiation and production of myelinating. Also, enhancement the effect of these exosomes through nasal administration to aging rat on myelination was seen [87].

As suggestion for MS treatment the dendritic cells used for extracting exosomes were stimulated with low-level IFNγ. Exosomes obtained from these cells contained microRNA species that could increase baseline myelination, reduce oxidative stress, also could improve remyelination in response to demyelination that happened by acute lysolecithin. In this study IFNγ-exosomes derived dendritic cell were administrated intranasally and selective uptake of these exosomes by oligodendrocytes was seen [88].

Glioblastoma (GBM) is one of the most lethal, aggressive and poorly-treated brain tumors. GBM could grow rapidly and consist various phenotypes of cells that constitute the glia (astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglia, ependymal cells) so the treatment of it is difficult. Gliomas are categorized in scale ranging base on the aggressiveness.[89] Surgical resection, postoperative radiotherapy, and chemotherapy are the gold standard for cancer treatment; hence in brain tumors, due to exists of BBB, the penetration of drugs molecules into the brain is limited [6]. Therefore, the effective drug delivery systems to treat this tumor has particular importance. For this purpose, exosomes, because of their specific properties, have been used to deliver genes and drugs to the brain to reduce the growth of tumor cells.

To overcome this disease the combination of several approaches has been suggested. To choose best treatment, finding biomarkers for monitoring patients in different stages could be very helpful. As exosomes are released from tumor cells and have effective messages on cellular and molecular pathways of other cells, their cargos (like: proteins, mRNA and miRNA) have especial roles in different stages of cancer. The exosomes and its contents could be suggested as diagnosis, prognosis and therapeutic biomarkers for cancers like brain cancers [90].

Yang and their colleagues isolated four types of exosomes derived from brain cells (Endothelial bEND.3 neuroectodermal tumor PFSK-1, glioblastoma A-172, and glioblastoma astrocytoma U-87 MG) and evaluated exosomes characterization, transport mechanism, and cell uptake. They loaded two potent chemotherapeutic drugs (Doxorubicin and Paclitaxel) in exosomes by incubation loading at 37 °C and their cytotoxicity on U-87 cell line were examined. The brain distribution and efficacy of exosomes formulation on brain cancer were investigated on Zebrafish model. They also in resent their study they used the brain endothelial bEND.3 exosomes, as they found from their previous work that these exosomes among other four brain cells exosomes could better function as carrier for brain delivery [91].

They loaded vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) siRNA in the brain endothelial bEND.3 exosomes with the assistance of Lipofectamine® 2000 transfection reagent. The inhibition effect of these exosomes in U-87 cells was observes at the expression of VEGF RNA and protein levels. Also these exosomes delivered VEGF siRNA decreased the fluorescent intensity of xenotransplanted cancer cells in the brain of zebrafish [92].

In another study doxorubicin was loaded in blood serum derived exosomes and macrophage derived exosomes to aim GMB therapy. For this purpose, focused ultrasound that is non-invasive and could reversibly, instantaneously, and locally open the BBB was utilized to more accumulation of exosomes in tumor cite [93].

As another example of exosome usage in cancer treatment, the anti-tumor effect of embryonic stem cells (ESCs) exosomes on GBM and their potential to carry of paclitaxel for GBM was investigated by Qingwei Zhu. The ESC-exosomes were modified by c(RGDyK) to enhance targeting so it was investigated paclitaxel has more curative effects when loaded in cRGD-exosomes of ESC [94].

In different research two methods of loading were suggested for paclitaxel. This therapeutical molecule was loaded in exosomes derived from U −87 cell by incubation and ultra-sonication methods [95,96].

In order to loading two chemical compounds in exosomes with the aim to improve target therapy and diagnosis of intracranial tumors, curcumin and super paramagnetic iron oxid nanoparticles (SPIONs) were applied. Loading of curcumin and SPIONs into exosomes was done by electroporation method and then exosomes were targeted by conjugating neuropilin-1 targeted peptide (RGERPPR,RGE) with exosome membrane through cyclo addition reaction of sulfonyl azide. After invitro and in vivo investigation glioblastoma cell and orthotopic xenograft glioma model in striatum of mice through IV administration, it resulted that these RGE exosomes could transfer their payload cross the BBB. Moreover, the synergistic antitumor effect of SPIONs and curcumin was achieved due to the magnetic flow hyperthermia of SPIONs [97].

Natural killer cells also were used to isolate exosomes for treatment of GBM exosomes derived from natural killer-92MI cells characterized by various methods and their cytokine levels were assessed. The apoptosis effect of these exosomes on U87 cells that transfected by enhanced firefly luciferase and thy 1 was seen through western blotting and flowcytometry analysis. To evaluate the anti-tumor effect of natural killer cells derived exosomes on xenograft mice, exosomes were injected intravenously and in order to decrease the liver uptake of exosomes, dextran sulfate was injected to mice intraperitoneally before treatment with natural killer cells extracted exosomes. The inhibition of tumor growth was greater in group treated with dextran and exosomes compared with exosomes alone and it was greater in mice given exosomes alone compared with control group. [98].
In a study the exosomes of human endometrial stem cells was loaded with atorvastatin to evaluate the cytotoxicity of this formulation on 3D glioblastoma model [99].

As neutrophils presented the BBB-crossing and inflammatory chemotactically responsibility properties its extracted exosomes were used as drug delivery systems to brain. Doxorubicin was loaded in this exosome to investigate on glioma models of zebrafish and C6-Luc glioma-bearing mice. In these models doxorubicin penetrated to brain rapidly owing to neutrophil-exosomes [100].

Exosomes has been introduced as nucleic-acid carriers, therefore to obtain more exosomal mRNA transcriptions the cellular nonoperation has been investigated. The mRNA-containing exosomes inhibited growth tumor in orthotopic phosphatase and tensin homologue deficient glioma mouse models [101].

Micro RNAs (miRNAs) could be transferred by exosomes so miRNAs like miR-146b which reduces GBM cell viability, invasion and migration was used to transfect marrow stromal cell. The exosomes harvested from these cells reduced glioma after intra-tumor injection in rat models [102].

Another miRNA that was studied for harnessing to treat GBM was miR-1 which has function in extracellular vesicles communication, and ectopic expression of it could blocked growth, neovascularization and in vasiveness of GBM [103].

F. M. Lang and their coworkers investigated 8 miRs to select the most potent antiglioma miRs among them so they found miR-124a had the most antiglioma effect against glioma stem cells. To obtain exosomes contain miR-124a bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cells was transfected with lentivirus vectors. Finally these miR-124a exosomes were tested in vitro and in vivo and their effects were significantly observed [104].

In other study also transfection of cells was utilized to obtain EVs with the aim to treatment of GBM. It was seen the suicide gene mRNA and protein (cytosine deaminase fused to uracil phosoribosyl transferase) delivered by EVs significantly reduce growth of xenograft GBM tumor in mice after administrated intratumorally injected [105].

The AMO-21 is antisense miRNA oligonucleotides against miR-21 which can use as therapeutic strategy for glioblastoma. In order to elevate targeting of AMO-21 on glioblastoma exosomes modified with transferrin receptor-binding peptide (T7) were utilized. To evaluate the efficiency of this targeting peptide, the RVG peptide-exosomes and unmodified exosomes were used as control. In both in vitro un C6 glioblastoma cells and rat models investigations the T7-exosomes delivered the AMO-21 more efficiently than other two groups [106].

Stroke is one of the leading causes of death and acquired disability worldwide. The block of blood vessels of brain decrease oxygen so brain functions arrest. This sickness has been noticed the older peoples but many factors such as unhealthy diet, stressful lifestyle and deteriorating environment quality have decreased the age of patients [107]. Since the only therapy after ischemic stroke has been used is thrombolytic therapy, development of innovative and effective treatment that can limit cascades of ischemia and associated pathology and have neuro-restorative (protective) effect could help to reduce ischemic lesion volume [108,109].
In this way many researches have been find and in several of them exosomes were chosen as the safe and effective vehicle to deliver drugs to brain for treatment of ischemic stroke.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical therapeutical molecules loaded in exosomes
ce:section.ce:para: Tian and coworkers prepared exosomes with c(RGDyK) peptide on their surface to increase the exosomes targeting capability so they detected modified exosomes in the lesion region of the ischemic brain of transient middle cerebral artery occlusion mice model after intravenous administration. Also they found the inflammatory response and cellular apoptosis in the lesion region reduced when curcumin loaded in c(RGDyK) conjugated exosomes were administrated [109].

In another research also curcumin as an anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective agent was used for ischemic injury treatment through exosome drug delivery. For this purpose, exosomes derived from embryonic stem cell and curcumin loaded exosomes were administrated intravenously to mice with ischemia reperfusion injury. It was investigated that edema and infarct volume were reduced after treatment with curcumin exosomes and expression of some markers were evaluated. As well the restoration of vascular endothelial tight and adherent junction proteins was observed in treated mice compared to untreated [108].
In exosomes derived from macrophages again curcumin was loaded to reduce accumulation of ROS and mitochondria-mediated neuronal apoptosis in ischemia [110].

Modification of exosomes by conjugation to a monoclonal antibody against growth-associated protein-43 (mAb GAP43) could improve neuron-targeting in ischemic stroke cases due to high expression of GAP43 in damaging or stimulating neurons. It was found the quercetin loaded in mAb GAP43-exosomes has more improvement effect on injury neurons in comparison with free quercetin and quercetin loaded in non-modified exosome. The quercetin-mAb GAP43-exosome expressed dual targeting effect by scavenging of ROS production in cerebral artery occlusion/reperfusion model [111].

Baicalin is an antioxidant and free radical scavenging flavonoid that were loaded in macrophage derived exosomes.in the transient middle cerebral artery occlusion/reperfusion model and permanent middle cerebral artery occlusion model, exosomal baicalin could arrived into brain better than free form of baicalin as well as reduction of ROS generation and activation of Nrf2/HO-1 pathway in neurons were better in presence of exosomal baicalin [112].

In addition to other exosome formulations prepared for ischemia, the iron oxide nanoparticles were used to improve targeting and therapeutic effects of exosomes extracted from mesenchymal stem cells [113].

The other route for neuroremodeling is alteration the gene and protein expression, therefore (accordingly) microRNAs (miRNA) that have efficient role in remodeling process through regulating target genes at post-transcriptional level have been utilized for neuro restoration caused by stroke. Exosomes as discussed before have potential to delivery of RNAs to CNS so they were harnessed to transfer miRNA (exosomes/mirnas as mediating cell base therapy of stroke). Accordingly, it was seen that modified (engineered) exosome with RVG fused to exosomal protein Lamp2b were utilized to transfer miR-124 to infarct site to promote neurogenesis [114].

Geng and their colleagues investigated exosomes extracted from adipose derived stem cells (ADSCs) that in one group miRNA-126 was over expressed and knockdown in ADSCs to prepare miR-126+ exosomes and miR-126- exosomes, respectively. They found miR-126+ exosomes in stroke rats significantly improve cell proliferation, neurogenesis and vasogenesis and decrease neuron apoptosis and inflammatory response compare to miR-126- exosomes. Further, it was seen recovery symptoms in a group that take naïve exosome was better than the group that received miRNA- exosomes [115].

To understand the role of pro-inflammatory factor interferon gamma (IFN-γ) on ischemic injuries the exosomes of human neural stem cells were isolated and stimulated by IFN-γ. The cell proliferation and survival were improved in in vitro study and the behavioral functions recovered in in vivo investigation trough administration of IFN-γ-exosomes compared to natural exosomes. The cell survival effect was attributed to specific exosomal miRNAs which were defined by next-generation sequencing [116].

Hongqi. X and their coworkers have studied various researches on multipotent mesenchymal stromal cells (MSC) derived exosomes. They have found these exosomes significantly have had effects on neurite remodeling, neurogenesis, angiogenesis, and increasing the axonal density and synaptophysin- positive areas on the ischemic zone, as well as effect improvement of functional recovery in stroked rats through IV administration of these exosomes compared with PBS injection [117]. They also studied MSC exosomes content especially miRNAs which have important role in tissue repairing and functional recovery after stroke [118].

In this way they investigated the effect (mechanism) of miR-133b on neurite outgrowth [119] by using knockin and knockdown technique to upregulate or downregulate miR-133b in MSCs and their extracted exosomes. Also, they treated stroked rats with miR-133b modified treatments by using knockin and knockdown methods. Finally, it was suggested neurite remodeling and functional recovery could be related to MSCs exosomes that mediated delivery of miR-133b to astrocytes and neurons [120]. The same research was done by this team about the miR-17–92 cluster around increases neural plasticity and functional recovery after stroke [121].

The angiogenesis of brain tissue after cerebral ischemia could improve by microRNA-210 so for targeting this RNA to transient middle cerebral artery occlusion mouse model the c(RGDyK) peptide conjugated exosome was used. After intravenous administration this targeted exosome the microRNA-210 was located in the lesion region of the ischemic brain thereupon the integrin β3, vascular endothelial growth factor and CD34 were increased [122].

Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a traumatic condition of CNS in which the limbs partially or completely miss their function below the initial site of injury. The disruption of signaling in neurons following by injury cause major dysfunction to the motor, sensory, and autonomic systems. THE initial traumatic injury in CNS triggers a cascade of degenerative process. For example the scar of glials after injury increases the cell death or upregulation of inhibitory factors could decrease recovery [123]. Though, the exact molecular mechanisms of neural repairing is unclear and due to the obstacles for crossing the BBB there is no effective treatment for SCI [124].

As the BBB prevent transportation of therapeutic molecules to CNS, repairing neurons after trauma of CNS is difficult. In a study by Yue Fan and coworkers on spinal cord injury (SCI), they prepared resveratrol exosomes through primary treating of microglia with resveratrol. Through in vitro and in vivo investigation the induction of autophagy and inhibition of apoptosis of neural cells via PI3K signaling pathway were attributed to resveratrol exosomes so this formulation could be introduced for recovery of neurons [124].

In SCI, immune activation of two phenotypes of macrophages/microglia is occurred. These types are pro-inflammatory classically activated phenotype and the anti-inflammatory alternatively activated phenotype. Exosomes of second type of macrophages were extracted and loaded with bererine. The reduction of inflammatory and apoptotic cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, Caspase 9, Caspase 8) and induction of polarization from type one phenotype of macrophages to type two was achieved by administration of berberine-exosomes in in vitro and in vivo studies [125].

Drug addiction poses serious social, medical and economic issues and large proportion of the population suffers from it. In last decades better findings about pathological changes of brain that promote substance abuse have been achieved. On the other hand, the existed treatments have disadvantages due to non-specific functions so represent poor acceptance and outcomes. This failure may be cause of brain structure to prevent the entrance of non-targeted systems [126].

Since exosome drug delivery could receive to CNS it was utilized for treat morphine addiction. In this way opioid receptor mu (MOR) siRNA was transfer with exosomes that the neuron specific RVG was expressed on their membrane. These RVG exosomes contained MOR siRNA reduce MOR mRNA and protein also inhibited morphine relapse by down regulation of MOR expression levels [127].

Because of the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems (NDDSs) have shown significant therapeutic potential in cancer and otherdiseases such as brain disorders [128]. Nanoparticles are frequently covalently bonded with targeted antibodies, peptides, or other biomolecules to enhance the ability of targeting anticancer drug delivery to malignancies and target tissues [129]. However, because of the increased immune-elimination, the existence of targeting ligands might possibly have a negative impact on nanoparticle distribution in the body [130]. Furthermore, since the receptors vary from the different biological or phenotypic heterogeneity of tumors, targeting these conjugated nanoparticles with targeting ligands is not feasible or accurate for a wide range of cancers or diseases [131].

Biomimetic nanoparticles, which merge the unique functions of natural biomaterials, such as cells or cell membranes, with the engineering diversity of synthetic nanoparticles, have lately received much interest as drug delivery systems [132]. Nanoparticles can be encapsulated by cancer cells,red blood cells (RBCs),platelets, or white blood cells (WBCs) and have shown perfectbioavailability, sustained circulation, and tumor-targeting capability [133-136]. Exosomes are small extracellular vesicles generated by mammalian cells that have recently become popular as nanocarriers due to their circulatory stability, bioactivity, minimal immunogenicity, and low cytotoxicity. Additionally, exosomes have excellent cellular absorption and target-homing characteristics that are reliant on membrane proteins [35,137,138]. Because exosome surface protein structure may be critical to their function, maintaining exosome membrane stability andintegrity and is critical for their use in drug platforms [139]. Exosomes can also eliminate waste in the nervous system, preserve the myelin sheath, and contribute in intercellular communication. Exosomes in the brain can also play a role in illnesses of the central nervous system, including stroke, Parkinson’s disease, traumatic encephalopathy (CTE),Alzheimer's disease andprion disease. In other hand, because exosomes can cross the BBBand are extremely stable in peripheral circulation, they can effectively protect disease-related molecules. As a result, using exosomes as a nanocarrierfor central nervous system diseases is a promising prospect, as they can be used to track disease progression and improve early detection and treatment [41].

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been widely used as nanomaterials for theranostic applications due to their diverse features in surface modification, imaging and therapeutics [140]. The unique functions of exosome-derived membranes were paired with synthesized AuNPs for targeted distribution to brain cells in a study by Khongkow et al (Fig. 4 ). Surface modification of AuNPs with brain-targeted exosomes obtained from genetically modified mammalian cells throughextrusion procedure. The AuNPs' targeting capabilities were confirmed by their binding to brain cells under laminar flow conditions as well as their improved transport across the BBBafter synthesis with brain-targeted exosomes. In vivo bioluminescence imaging indicated that targeted-exosome coated AuNPs gathered in the mouse brain following intravenous administration, demonstrating their potential to identify brain cells. The surface alteration of synthetic AuNPs with the brain-targeted exosome reported in this study was a successful technique for brain targeting, and it holds promise for the future use of modified AuNPs to penetrate the brain [141].

Several obstacles stand in the way of therapeutic development, including a lack of data on exosome trafficking and biodistribution and the difficulties of imaging deep brain areas in vivo [142]. By using computed tomography imaging and glucose-coated Au NPslabeling, Betzer et al developed a system for non-invasive in vivo neuroimaging and exosome tracking of brain related exosomes. Exosomes were directly labeled with Au NPs through parent cells, rather than the more common and inefficient indirect labeling method. On a molecular level, they observed that glucose-coated Au NPswere up taken into MSC-derived exosomes via an active, energy-dependent pathway, mediated by the glucose transporter GLUT-1 andinvolving endocytic proteins. best size and delivery route was evaluated and data showed that5 nm Au NPsallowed for better exosome labeling and that intranasal treatment, as opposed to intravenous, resulted in superior brain deposition and hence enhanced in vivo neuroimaging. They also used a mouse model of localized brain ischemia to track intranasally delivered AuNP-labeled exosomes, which revealed greater accumulation at the lesion site over 24 h when compared to non-specific migration and clearance from control brains. This exosome labeling technology can be used to diagnose a variety of brain illnesses and could potentially improve exosome-based neuronaltherapy [142].

Perets et al. recently developed an approachfor quantitative andlongitudinalin vivo exosome neuroimaging based on the superior viewing powers of Au NPsas labeling agents andX-ray computed tomography (CT) method. Intranasally administered exosomes produced from bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (MSC-exo) were utilized to track the homing patterns andmigration in several brain diseases, including Alzheimer's, autism, stroke andParkinson. MSC-exo was found to be precisely targeted and deposited in pathologically important brain areas of mouse models up to 96 h after treatment, whereas healthy controls displayed a diffuse migratory pattern and clearance by 24 h. The signal of neuro-inflammatory was strongly correlated with accumulation of MSC-exo in diseased brains with an inflammatory-driven mechanism. Also, MSC-exo was uptaken preferentially with neuronal cells in the diseased locations and don’t uptaken by glial cells [143].

In another study, Niu et al developed doxorubicin-loaded heparin-based nanoparticles (DNs) and immobilized on the surface of natural grapefruit extracellular vehicles (EVs), enabling efficient drug administration and thereby considerably improving anti-glioma effectiveness. When compared to standard encapsulation for EVs, the patching method enabledfor an unparalleled 4-fold drug loading. By membrane fusion and receptor-mediated transcytosis, biomimetic EV-DNs were able to circumvent BBB/BBTB and infiltrate into glioma tissues, dramatically antiproliferation capability and increasing cellular internalization while also extending circulation duration. Of course, high-abundance deposit of EV-DNs in glioma tissues allows for maximum EV-DN absorption and antiglioma activity in vivo [144].

The most important disadvantage of employing exosomes for ischemic stroke is that they are not well targeted on the ischemic brain lesion after systemic injection, resulting in a poor treatment efficacy [109]. In a study, Kim et al. demonstratedthat magnetic NV (MNV) produced from iron oxide nanoparticles (IONP)-harboring MSC can enhance ischemic-lesion targeting and treatment success dramatically. Because IONP increased the expression of therapeutic growth factors in MSC, MNV had more of these therapeutic molecules than NV produced from naive MSCs. Magnetic guidance enhanced MNV positioning to the ischemic lesion by 5.1 times after systemic injection of MNV into(MCAO)-induced rats. The MNV administration and subsequent magnetic navigating in the ischemic brain lesion enhanced anti-inflammatory reaction, angiogenesis, and anti-apoptosis, resulting in a significantly reduced infarction volume and enhanced motor function [113].

As previously stated, advancing the clinical application of exosome-based therapeutics necessitates the understanding of key issues such as exosome biodistribution, tumor targeting and absorption, and the overcoming tumor barriers that prevent various nano-carriers and drugs from penetrating. Cohen et allooked at these factors in exosomes produced from and the A431 squamous cell carcinoma line (A431-exo) and mesenchymal stem cells (MSC-exo), both of which could be used to treat cancer. They continuously and quantitatively monitored the two intravenously injected exosome types in A431 tumor-bearing mice using a combining technique ofnon-invasive computed tomography imaging (CT) and Au NPstagging of exosomes. Both exosome forms had tumor homing capabilities, according to CT imaging up to 48 h and subsequent ex vivo study, however MSC-exo had much more tumor accumulation than A431-exo. MSC-exo was also able to penetrate the tumor and disperse throughout its bulk, whereas non-encapsulated Au NPs stayedconcentrated at the tumor's periphery. MSC-exo was found to penetrate not only the tumor tissue, but also the cytoplasm of tumor cells, according to histological investigation. While both exosome types had equal proportions of biodistribution amongst organs at 48 h, A431-exosomes had a faster clearance rate [145].

The unspecific binding of lipophilic tracers to other proteins, the limits of fluorescence for deep tissue photography, and the impact of external labeling techniques on their natural tropism make analyzing the capacity of EVs to target specific cells challenging. Using fluorescence analysis and quantitative gold labeling assessments, Lara et al confirmed the cell-type specific tropism of B16F10-EVs towards cancer cells and metastatic tumors (Fig. 5 ). Without influencing size distribution, polydispersity, surface charge, protein markers, cell uptake, or in vivo biodistribution, surface functionalization of plasmonic Au NPswas employed to facilitate indirect labeling of EVs. Fluorescence/computer tomography imaging, quantitative neutron activation analysis, and gold-enhanced optical microscopy were used to examine double-labeled EVs with gold and fluorescent dyes injected into mice developing metastatic lung tumors. When compared to colon adenocarcinoma, macrophage, and kidney cell-derived EVs, B16F10 cells preferentially take up their own EVs. Furthermore, when compared to the rest of the organs, histological investigation revealed a preferential concentration of B16F10 EVs in tiny metastatic tumors in the lungs [146].

Exosome membrane structure and core content are mostly influenced by the cell of origin, and careful analysis of exosomal BBBpermeability and lipid bilayer composition is required [147]. To achieve these goals, For the investigation of purified human plasma-derived exosome-like vesicles, Jakubec et al developed a 3D static BBBsystem based on existing liposome systems and a complementing LC-MS/MS and 31P nuclear magnetic resonance approach. The isolated vesicles penetrate through the BBBand are picked up by endothelial cells, according to the findings. The separated vesicles are rich in lyso phospholipids and lack phosphatidylserine, according to the compositional study. These results differ significantly from the structure of exosomes derived from cell culture, and could indicate active macrophage clearance in response to exposed phosphahtidylserine [147].

Gold-carbon quantum dots (GCDs) are uniquefluorescent nanostructureswith good biocompatibility that can be easily manufactured using a microwave-assisted technique. GCDs can be used to image biological targets such as cells, exosomes, and other organelles due to their small size and interesting physicochemical features. Jiang et al employed GCDs to image exosomes fluorescence. Exosome-specific nanoprobes are formed by attaching tumor-specific antibodies to GCDs. Exosomes can be labeled with nanoprobes by immuno-reactions, allowing for fluorescence imaging of exosomes. Exosomes labeled with nanoprobes can be taken up by live cells when treated with them. The most of exosomes were endocytosed by cells and delivered to lysosomes, according to the intracellular investigations. The GCDs have no effect on the way exosomes are taken up or their intracellular distribution. The findings of the experiments showed that the nanoprobe can be utilized to investigate the intrinsic intracellular behavior of tumor-derived exosomes [148].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges for drug delivery by exosomes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve exosomes as drug delivery systems for brain disorders some challenges have to overcome. One of them is large-scale production of exosomes at low cost with standard isolation techniques to obtain high purity exosomes. Methods of drug loading into exosomes has an important role in preparation of these drug delivery systems due to the presence of various biomolecules in exosomes. Hence, loading external chemical or biomolecules into exosomes is another challenging. The composition of exosomes has another problem for transferring undesirable content derived from the generating cells which can be immunogenic and oncogenic [37,149]. Also, in research the challenges facing the commercialization of exosomes were mentioned as technical, economic and regulatory issues [150]. In this regard many studies have been done by different researcher for solving the problems to achieve the exosomes as drug delivery systems in clinical uses.

Exosomes are endocytic-derived extracellular vesicles that are released by cells and can be found in biofluids such as saliva, urine, and plasma. Small RNA is abundant in these vesicles, which play a role in a variety of physiological processes. They play a role in synaptic plasticity, neuronal stress response, cell-to-cell communication, and neurogenesis in the brain. Exosomes, in particular, have a number of advantages over other nanoparticles, including excellent biocompatibility, low immunogenicity, high stability, a long half-life, the ability to cross physical barriers like the BBB, and targetability. Its ability to deliver functional biomolecules (therapeutic proteins, chemotherapy, and nucleic acids) as well as propensity for bioengineering has also attracted significant attention in the past few years. For example, different inorganic nanoparticles such as quantum dots, Au, iron oxide hollow Au, V2C quantum dots, Ag2Se@Mn with size ranging from 2 to 40 nm loaded exosomes originated from body fluids, MCF-7celles, HEK293T cells, B16-F10 cells, MCF-7 cells, Human MSCs were explored for various applications including imaging and therapy. Encapsulating therapeutic compounds into inorganic nanoparticles or directly employing inorganic nanoparticles to induce photothermal therapy were both used to achieve the therapeutic effect. Exosomes originating from human heptacarcinoma BeI7402 cells were successfully loaded with doxorubicin-loadedsilicon porous nanoparticles. Because of the profound tissue penetration of NIR, inorganic nanoparticles with NIR absorption have demonstrated to be an excellent photothermal therapeutic method. Exosome tracking with fluorescence imaging was made possible by silicon's natural fluorescent characteristics, and doxorubicin molecules trapped inside silicon's pores provided therapeutic effects. It has demonstrated excellent anticancer activity, which can be related to cancer stem cell targeting and apoptosis. Inorganic nanoparticles hollow gold particles with a diameter of 40 nm act as anticancer agents loaded by MSC-derived exosomes for drug encapsulation. If inorganic nanoparticles are small enough (∼3 nm), electroporation may be a useful approach for loading nanoparticles into exosomes. Exosome research is focusing more on biomarkers and their ability to mediate cell-to-cell communication in the nervous system right now. In the meantime, more research is needed to determine the mechanism for bidirectional exosome transport across the BBB, changes in exosome secretion or size, cargo composition changes, as well as cell type-specific differences that can be identified. As a result of their ability to reverse physiological changes in nervous system disorders, exosomes derived from CNS cells have great biomarker potential. Of course, more research is needed to determine which cells should be used for manufacturing, as well as a new approach to increase bioactive molecule loading efficiency without compromising exosome integrity.
